# Numbers Chapter 15 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 166-167 | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_015_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/NUM_015_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0123-0124 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_015_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/NUM_015_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

### Laws of Offerings in the Land (vv. 1-16)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**2** "Speak to the Sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land of your dwelling places which I am giving to you,

**3** and you make an offering by fire to YHWH — a burnt offering or a sacrifice, to fulfill a vow, or as a freewill offering, or in your appointed feasts, to make a pleasing aroma to YHWH, from the herd or from the flock —

**4** then the one who brings his offering shall present to YHWH a grain offering of one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour, mixed with one-fourth of a hin of oil.

**5** And wine for the drink offering, one-fourth of a hin, you shall prepare with the burnt offering or for the sacrifice, for each lamb.

**6** Or for a ram, you shall prepare a grain offering of two-tenths of an ephah of fine flour, mixed with one-third of a hin of oil.

**7** And wine for the drink offering, one-third of a hin, you shall present as a pleasing aroma to YHWH.

**8** And when you prepare a bull as a burnt offering or a sacrifice, to fulfill a vow, or as a peace offering to YHWH,

**9** then he shall present with the bull a grain offering of three-tenths of an ephah of fine flour, mixed with one-half of a hin of oil.

**10** And wine you shall present for the drink offering, one-half of a hin — an offering by fire, a pleasing aroma to YHWH.

**11** Thus shall it be done for each bull, or for each ram, or for each of the male lambs or of the goats.

**12** According to the number that you prepare, thus shall you do for each one, according to their number.

**13** Every native shall do these things in this manner, to present an offering by fire, a pleasing aroma to YHWH.

**14** And when a sojourner sojourns with you, or whoever is among you throughout your generations, and he makes an offering by fire, a pleasing aroma to YHWH — as you do, so shall he do.

**15** As for the assembly, there shall be one statute for you and for the sojourner who sojourns with you, a perpetual statute throughout your generations. As you are, so shall the sojourner be before YHWH.

**16** One law and one ordinance shall be for you and for the sojourner who sojourns with you."

---

### The Offering of the First of the Dough (vv. 17-21)

**17** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**18** "Speak to the Sons of Israel and say to them: When you come into the land to which I am bringing you,

**19** then it shall be that when you eat of the bread of the land, you shall present a contribution to YHWH.

**20** Of the first of your dough you shall present a cake as a contribution; as the contribution of the threshing floor, so shall you present it.

**21** Of the first of your dough you shall give to YHWH a contribution throughout your generations.

---

### Sin Offerings for Unintentional Transgressions (vv. 22-29)

**22** And when you err and do not observe all these commandments which YHWH has spoken to Moses —

**23** all that YHWH has commanded you by the hand of Moses, from the day that YHWH commanded and onward throughout your generations —

**24** then it shall be, if it was done unintentionally, away from the eyes of the congregation, that the whole congregation shall offer one bull of the herd as a burnt offering, for a pleasing aroma to YHWH, with its grain offering and its drink offering according to the ordinance, and one male goat as a sin offering.

**25** And the priest shall make atonement for the whole congregation of the Sons of Israel, and it shall be forgiven them, for it was an error. And they shall bring their offering, an offering by fire to YHWH, and their sin offering before YHWH, for their error.

**26** And it shall be forgiven the whole congregation of the Sons of Israel and the sojourner who sojourns among them, for with respect to the whole people it was done in error.

**27** And if one person sins unintentionally, he shall present a female goat in its first year as a sin offering.

**28** And the priest shall make atonement before YHWH for the person who erred, when he sins unintentionally, to make atonement for him, and it shall be forgiven him.

**29** For the native among the Sons of Israel and for the sojourner who sojourns among them — one law shall be for you, for the one who acts unintentionally.

---

### The Sin of the High Hand (vv. 30-31)

**30** But the person who acts with a high hand, whether native or sojourner, he blasphemes YHWH, and that person shall be cut off from the midst of his people.

**31** For the word of YHWH he has despised, and his commandment he has broken. That person shall surely be cut off; his iniquity is upon him."

---

### The Sabbath Breaker (vv. 32-36)

**32** And while the Sons of Israel were in the wilderness, they found a man gathering wood on the Sabbath day.

**33** And those who found him gathering wood brought him to Moses and to Aaron and to the whole congregation.

**34** And they placed him in custody, for it had not been declared what should be done to him.

**35** And YHWH said to Moses, "The man shall surely be put to death. The whole congregation shall stone him with stones outside the camp."

**36** And the whole congregation brought him outside the camp and stoned him with stones, and he died, just as YHWH had commanded Moses.

---

### The Commandment of the Tassels (vv. 37-41)

**37** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying,

**38** "Speak to the Sons of Israel and say to them that they shall make for themselves tassels on the corners of their garments throughout their generations, and they shall put on the tassel of each corner a cord of blue.

**39** And it shall be a tassel for you, and you shall see it and remember all the commandments of YHWH and do them, and you shall not seek after your own heart and your own eyes, after which you go whoring.

**40** So that you may remember and do all my commandments, and be holy to your God.

**41** I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt to be your God. I am YHWH your God."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Notes

1. **Verse 2 -- "When you come into the land" (כִּי תָבֹאוּ אֶל־אֶרֶץ מוֹשְׁבֹתֵיכֶם):** The placement of this chapter immediately after the devastating judgment of chapter 14, where the current generation is condemned to die in the wilderness, is striking. The phrase "the land of your dwelling places" (erets moshevoteikhem) addresses a future generation and is thus an implicit promise: despite the judgment upon the present generation, YHWH's purpose to bring Israel into the land remains firm. The LXX (Vaticanus pa_0123) renders εις την γην της κατοικησεως υμων (into the land of your habitation). The word moshevot (dwelling places) from the root yashav (to sit, to dwell, to inhabit) emphasizes settled habitation, not mere entry. This is a word of hope embedded within a legal section.

2. **Verse 3 -- "an offering by fire" (אִשֶּׁה לַיהוָה):** The term isheh (offering by fire) from the root אשׁ (fire) is the general category that encompasses burnt offerings (olah), sacrifices/peace offerings (zevach), vow offerings (neder), and freewill offerings (nedavah). The phrase "pleasing aroma" (re'ach nichoach) is anthropomorphic language describing YHWH's favorable acceptance of the offering (cf. Genesis 8:21; Exodus 29:18). The LXX renders καρπωμα κυριω (a fruit-offering to the Lord) and later οσμην ευωδιας (a sweet-smelling odor).

3. **Verses 4-10 -- Graduated Offering Quantities:** The grain and drink offering quantities are calibrated to the size of the animal sacrifice: (a) for a lamb -- one-tenth ephah of flour, one-fourth hin of oil, one-fourth hin of wine; (b) for a ram -- two-tenths ephah of flour, one-third hin of oil, one-third hin of wine; (c) for a bull -- three-tenths ephah of flour, one-half hin of oil, one-half hin of wine. The hin (approximately six liters) is subdivided into halves, thirds, and fourths. The ephah (approximately twenty-two liters) is divided into tenths. The proportional system reflects the principle that greater offerings require proportionally greater accompaniments. The Leningrad Codex (page 166, right column) presents these quantities with the standard Masoretic vocalization. The LXX preserves the same proportional system with Greek equivalents.

4. **Verses 13-16 -- "One law...for you and for the sojourner" (תּוֹרָה אַחַת וּמִשְׁפָּט אֶחָד):** The legal equality of the native-born Israelite (ezrach) and the sojourner (ger) before YHWH is a distinctive feature of Israelite law. The ger is not a casual visitor but a resident alien who has attached himself to Israel's community. The phrase torah achat umishpat echad (one law and one ordinance) establishes a single legal standard governing sacrificial worship. This principle appears also in Exodus 12:49, Leviticus 24:22, and Numbers 9:14. The LXX renders νομος εις εσται υμιν και τω προσηλυτω (one law shall be for you and for the proselyte), using the significant term προσηλυτος (proselyte, one who has come near), which carries a stronger connotation of religious conversion than the Hebrew ger (sojourner, resident alien).

5. **Verses 17-21 -- "The first of your dough" (רֵאשִׁית עֲרִסֹתֵכֶם):** The word arisah (dough, coarse meal) occurs only here and in Ezekiel 44:30 and Nehemiah 10:38 in the Hebrew Bible. The reshit (first, firstfruits, beginning) of the dough is to be given as a terumah (contribution, heave offering) to YHWH. The terumah is that portion "lifted up" or "set apart" from the whole for sacred use. The comparison to the terumah of the threshing floor (terumah goren) equates the processed grain product (dough) with the raw grain product at harvest. The LXX renders απαρχην φυραματος υμων (firstfruits of your dough). This law introduces a domestic observance: the setting apart of the first portion of bread-making, which was to be given to the priest (cf. Ezekiel 44:30). The Hebrew challah (cake, loaf) in verse 20 is the term from which the traditional Jewish Sabbath bread takes its name.

6. **Verses 22-26 -- Congregational Sin Offering for Unintentional Error:** The phrase "when you err" (ki tishgu) uses the verb shagah (to err, to go astray, to wander from the path), denoting inadvertent deviation from the commandments rather than deliberate defiance. The expression "away from the eyes of the congregation" (me-einei ha-edah) suggests the error was not publicly recognized at first. The offering prescribed is both a burnt offering (olah -- one bull) and a sin offering (chattat -- one male goat), with the standard grain and drink offerings. The dual offering for communal sin is more elaborate than the individual offering (v. 27 -- one female goat). The priestly atonement (kipper) effects forgiveness (nislach lahem). The LXX on Vaticanus pa_0123-0124 follows the MT closely, rendering αφεθησεται (it shall be forgiven). The verb salach/nislach (to forgive) in verses 25-26 is used exclusively of divine forgiveness, as noted in the chapter 14 notes.

7. **Verse 27 -- "a female goat in its first year" (עֵז בַּת־שְׁנָתָהּ):** The individual sin offering for unintentional sin is a female goat (ez bat shenatah, literally "a she-goat, daughter of her year"), meaning a yearling. This differs from Leviticus 4:27-28, which prescribes a female goat without specifying the age. The specification "in its first year" here may indicate a smaller, less costly animal appropriate for an individual rather than a community. The LXX renders αιγα εξ αιγων ενιαυσιαν (a she-goat of the goats, a yearling).

8. **Verses 30-31 -- "with a high hand" (בְּיָד רָמָה):** The phrase beyad ramah (with a high hand, with an uplifted hand) is the antithesis of the unintentional sin described in verses 22-29. It denotes deliberate, defiant, presumptuously willful sin -- a raised fist against YHWH. The same phrase is used in Exodus 14:8 to describe Israel's departure from Egypt "with a high hand" (i.e., boldly, defiantly), but here the defiance is directed against YHWH himself. Such a person "blasphemes YHWH" (et YHWH hu megaddeph), using the verb giddeph (to revile, to blaspheme). The consequence is being "cut off" (nikretah), the penalty of karet -- excision from the covenant community. The infinitive absolute construction "shall surely be cut off" (hikaret tikkaret) intensifies the certainty of the punishment. The phrase "his iniquity is upon him" (avonah bah) means the guilt remains upon the sinner, unatoned and unremoved. There is no sacrificial remedy for the sin of the high hand; it places the sinner beyond the reach of the cultic system. The LXX renders εν χειρι υπερηφανιας (with a hand of arrogance) and ψυχη εκεινη εξολεθρευθησεται (that soul shall be utterly destroyed).

9. **Verses 32-36 -- The Sabbath-Breaker:** This narrative is the sole case-law narrative between Sinai and the plains of Moab, and it functions as an illustration of the "sin with a high hand" just described in verses 30-31. The man was "gathering wood" (meqoshesh etsim) on the Sabbath day, a violation of the prohibition of work on the Sabbath (Exodus 20:8-11; 31:14-15; 35:2-3). The verb qoshesh (to gather, to collect) appears only here in this form. The detail that "it had not been declared what should be done to him" (ki lo forash mah ye'aseh lo) suggests that while the general penalty for Sabbath violation was known (death, Exodus 31:14-15), the specific mode of execution had not yet been specified. YHWH's instruction specifies stoning (seqilah) outside the camp. The LXX on Vaticanus pa_0124 renders ου γαρ συνεκριθη τι ποιησωσιν αυτον (for it had not been determined what they should do to him). The execution "outside the camp" (michutz lamachaneh) removes the source of impurity from the sacred space. Rabbinic tradition (Shabbat 96b) debated whether the man's sin was ignorance of the Sabbath law or deliberate defiance; the literary placement after verses 30-31 suggests the latter.

10. **Verses 37-41 -- The Tassels (Tzitzit):** The Hebrew term tzitzit (tassel, fringe) refers to twisted threads attached to the corners (kanfot, literally "wings") of the outer garment. The instruction to include a petil tekhelet (cord of blue) specifies a thread dyed with the precious blue-violet dye derived from the murex snail (Hexaplex trunculus). The tekhelet dye was associated with royalty, priesthood, and the tabernacle fabrics (Exodus 25:4; 26:1; 28:31). The theological purpose is explicitly stated: the tzitzit serves as a visual memorial to "remember all the commandments of YHWH and do them" (uzekhartem et kol mitsvot YHWH va-asitem otam). The warning against following "your own heart and your own eyes" (acharei levavkhem ve-acharei eineikhem) uses the verb zanah (to go whoring), the prophetic metaphor for idolatry and covenant unfaithfulness (cf. Exodus 34:15-16; Judges 2:17; Hosea 1-3). The LXX renders κρασπεδα (borders, edges) for tzitzit and κλωσμα υακινθινον (a hyacinthine thread) for petil tekhelet. The closing formula "I am YHWH your God" (ani YHWH eloheikhem) appears twice in verse 41, framing the exodus reference and creating an inclusio with verse 2. This formula is the self-identification of the covenant God, grounding the commandment in the relationship established at the exodus.

### Structural Observations

- Numbers 15 is strategically placed after the catastrophic rebellion and judgment of chapters 13-14. Its laws presuppose entry into the land ("when you come into the land," vv. 2, 18), which serves as an assurance that YHWH's purposes have not been defeated by human sin. The generation addressed is the next generation -- the children who will inherit what their parents rejected. The juxtaposition of judgment (ch. 14) with future-oriented legislation (ch. 15) embodies the tension between divine wrath and divine faithfulness that pervades the book of Numbers.

- The chapter has a chiastic theological structure: laws for the land (vv. 1-16), firstfruits of the dough (vv. 17-21), inadvertent sin and atonement (vv. 22-29), the defiant sinner and the Sabbath-breaker (vv. 30-36), and the tassels as a reminder against sin (vv. 37-41). The movement is from offering to YHWH, through the problem of sin, to a practical means of remembering obedience. The offerings bracket sin; the tassels guard against it.

- The legal equality of the native and the sojourner (vv. 14-16, 26, 29-30) is a unifying theme. The phrase "one law" (torah achat) occurs at the beginning (v. 16) and is echoed in verse 29. The sojourner participates fully in both the privileges (offering to YHWH) and the obligations (sin offerings, the penalty for high-handed sin) of the covenant community. This egalitarian principle anticipates the prophetic vision of the nations drawn to YHWH's worship (Isaiah 56:6-7).

- The contrast between inadvertent sin (vv. 22-29) and high-handed sin (vv. 30-31) is the theological pivot of the chapter. For inadvertent sin, atonement is available through the sacrificial system; for deliberate defiance, there is only karet (cutting off). The Sabbath-breaker narrative (vv. 32-36) illustrates the latter category. This distinction is foundational to the entire Levitical system and is echoed in Hebrews 10:26-27.

- The chapter concludes with the tzitzit commandment (vv. 37-41), which provides a tangible, daily reminder of all the commandments. The cord of blue (petil tekhelet) visually links the ordinary Israelite to the sacred realm of the tabernacle, where blue was the predominant color. Every Israelite wearing tzitzit carries a visible sign of covenant identity and obligation. The closing "I am YHWH your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt" echoes the opening of the Decalogue (Exodus 20:2), binding the tassels commandment to the entire covenantal framework.

- The Vaticanus LXX text (pa_0123 to pa_0124) follows the MT closely throughout Numbers 15, with the most notable divergence being the consistent use of προσηλυτος (proselyte) for the Hebrew ger (sojourner), reflecting the later Second Temple understanding of non-Israelites who join the community. The chapter division in Vaticanus shows the transition from ΛΖ (chapter 37 in the LXX numbering system) at the top of pa_0123 into the content of Numbers 15. The uncial script is clear and the text is well-preserved on both pages, with standard nomina sacra (ΚΣ for κυριος, ΘΣ for θεος) throughout.

---
*Translated by LOGOS from the Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 166-167, with reference to Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pa_0123-0124.*
